skill.name: when-mapping-dependencies-use-dependency-mapper
skill.description: Maps project dependency trees across package managers, flags dependency risks, and prepares reports or visualizations.
skill.summary: Analyze dependencies, security status, and graph structure across software projects.
skill.author: Claude Code

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced many false positives, including weak-crypto and Windows SAM detections caused by documentation text or non-security cache hashing. Human review confirmed elevated risk from an executable shell wrapper that runs external npx commands, plus documented network API calls, environment token use, filesystem cache writes, and an unsanitized HTML tooltip pattern. No prompt injection or confirmed malicious intent was found, so this is not blocked, but it should not be published without review and hardening.

high_findings.title: Runtime Execution of External Alpha Package
high_findings.description: The executable slash command invokes npx claude-flow@alpha hooks during initialization and finalization. npx may download and execute package code from the npm registry, which creates supply-chain and arbitrary code execution risk for users running the skill.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The shell file is executable behavior, not only documentation, and it directly runs npx with an alpha package. The commands are quoted and appear functional, so the risk is supply-chain execution rather than command injection.

medium_findings.title: Network Vulnerability Queries Can Transmit Project Data and Tokens
medium_findings.description: The implementation guide shows posting package data to the Snyk API with process.env.SNYK_TOKEN and describes querying multiple vulnerability databases. This is legitimate for dependency auditing, but it requires explicit user consent and secret handling controls.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documented code clearly sends package data to a third-party API with a token from the environment. It is purpose-aligned, but still creates privacy and credential-handling risk.

medium_findings.title: Generated HTML Uses Unsanitized innerHTML
medium_findings.description: The visualization example writes dependency metadata into tooltip.innerHTML. If graph data contains attacker-controlled package names or metadata, the generated report could expose users to XSS when opened in a browser.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The innerHTML assignment is explicit and uses values from graph metadata. The file is an implementation guide, so exploitability depends on whether this code is copied into a working renderer.

medium_findings.title: Filesystem Cache and Report Writes Need Clear Boundaries
medium_findings.description: The slash command stores cache files under the user home directory and writes reports to a caller-provided output directory. Paths are quoted, but the skill should document write locations and avoid overwriting existing files without user confirmation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file accesses are real and mostly expected for caching and output generation. The risk is moderate because user-controlled output paths are quoted and there is no evidence of sensitive file targeting.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto Detections Are Mostly False Positives
low_findings.description: Most weak-cryptography findings point to ordinary documentation lines, JSON descriptions, or example text. The confirmed md5sum usage is only a cache key for project paths, not password storage, signing, or integrity protection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only concrete hash command is md5sum for cache naming. Other cited weak-crypto locations do not contain security-sensitive cryptographic use.

low_findings.title: Sensitive Windows SAM Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported Windows SAM database access in the shell script, but review found no Windows SAM path access or credential database reads. The flagged locations are option parsing, task text, and output formatting.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines reference INCLUDE_SECURITY, command output, or generated task instructions. No evidence found of reading Windows SAM files or credential stores.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Documentation URLs Are Low Risk
low_findings.description: The hardcoded repository and documentation URLs are metadata, not data exfiltration endpoints. They may be inaccurate or unaffiliated, but they do not execute code by themselves.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear in metadata fields named repository and documentation. There is no surrounding code that posts data to these URLs.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Command Execution Through npx
dangerous_patterns.description: The shell wrapper executes npx claude-flow@alpha hooks, which can run registry-provided code at runtime.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is present in executable shell code and executes an external package command.

dangerous_patterns.title: Environment Token Sent to Third-Party API
dangerous_patterns.description: The Snyk example constructs an Authorization header from process.env.SNYK_TOKEN for a remote API request.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The data flow from environment token to fetch headers is explicit, though it appears to be a legitimate vulnerability-scanning integration.

dangerous_patterns.title: Unsanitized HTML Injection Sink
dangerous_patterns.description: The HTML visualization example assigns dependency metadata to tooltip.innerHTML.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The sink is clear, but this file is documentation-style implementation guidance rather than a bundled runtime renderer.

content.value_statement: Large dependency trees hide security, version, and architecture risks. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through dependency discovery, graph analysis, and actionable reporting.
actual_capabilities: Guides analysis for npm, yarn, pnpm, pip, poetry, cargo, maven, Go modules, bundler, composer, and nuget projects.
Defines workflows for reading manifests and lock files, then building direct and transitive dependency trees.
Describes checks for circular dependencies, duplicate packages, outdated versions, and dependency depth.
Includes security-audit guidance using npm audit, Snyk, GitHub advisories, CVE sources, and OSV.
Provides report and visualization patterns for console, JSON, HTML, SVG, and GraphViz outputs.
Includes a slash-command wrapper that prepares agent instructions and coordinates Claude-Flow hooks.
limitations: The included shell command mostly simulates analysis output and depends on agent implementation for real results.
Security scans can require network access and third-party API tokens.
Vulnerability and license results depend on external databases and may be incomplete or outdated.
HTML visualization examples need sanitization before use with untrusted dependency metadata.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Release Dependency Audit
use_cases.description: Review direct and transitive packages before a release, with focus on high-risk vulnerabilities and outdated dependencies.

use_cases.title: Investigate Architecture Cycles
use_cases.description: Map dependency paths to find circular references, duplicated packages, and deep transitive chains that complicate maintenance.

use_cases.title: Create Supply Chain Reports
use_cases.description: Generate dependency summaries that combine package counts, security status, license checks, and prioritized remediation tasks.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Dependency Map
prompt_templates.prompt: Use dependency-mapper to analyze this project. Identify the package manager, direct dependencies, transitive dependency count, and the most important risks.

prompt_templates.title: Security-Focused Audit
prompt_templates.prompt: Use dependency-mapper with security analysis for this repository. Prioritize critical and high vulnerabilities, affected packages, and practical upgrade steps.

prompt_templates.title: Architecture Dependency Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Use dependency-mapper to find circular dependencies, duplicate packages, deep dependency chains, and dependency groups that should be simplified.

prompt_templates.title: Full Supply Chain Report
prompt_templates.prompt: Use dependency-mapper to produce a full dependency risk report with package-manager detection, vulnerability sources, license concerns, outdated packages, graph insights, and prioritized remediation.

output_examples.input: Analyze a Node.js project before release.
output: Detected npm with one manifest and one lock file.
Found 23 direct dependencies and 809 transitive dependencies.
Reported two high-severity vulnerabilities and recommended targeted package updates.

output_examples.input: Review dependency architecture for a service with build conflicts.
output: Highlighted duplicate package versions that may cause runtime mismatch.
Flagged a deep transitive chain that should be reviewed.
Provided a dependency graph summary for maintainers.

output_examples.input: Prepare an executive dependency health summary.
output: Summarized total dependency volume, security status, outdated packages, and license concerns.
Separated urgent fixes from longer-term cleanup tasks.
Listed assumptions and external data sources used during the review.

best_practices: Run the skill on a clean working tree so generated reports are easy to review.
Confirm before enabling network-based vulnerability checks or sending package data to third-party services.
Treat generated remediation steps as a review queue, then verify changes with the native package manager.
anti_patterns: Do not run the shell wrapper in sensitive repositories without reviewing its npx and filesystem behavior.
Do not rely on simulated output as proof that a real dependency audit was completed.
Do not publish generated HTML reports from untrusted metadata without sanitizing rendered fields.

faq.question: Which package managers does this skill describe?
faq.answer: It covers npm, yarn, pnpm, pip, poetry, pipenv, cargo, maven, gradle, Go modules, bundler, composer, and nuget.

faq.question: Does it perform real vulnerability scanning by itself?
faq.answer: The documentation defines vulnerability-scanning workflows. The included shell wrapper mainly prepares instructions and simulated output.

faq.question: Does it require network access?
faq.answer: Network access is needed for current vulnerability data from services such as Snyk, GitHub advisories, CVE sources, or OSV.

faq.question: Can it generate visual dependency graphs?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill describes HTML, SVG, GraphViz, and console visualizations for dependency trees and risk highlights.

faq.question: What should users review before running it?
faq.answer: Review the npx claude-flow commands, output directory, cache directory, and any third-party API token usage.

faq.question: Is this skill safe for sensitive projects?
faq.answer: Use caution. It is not blocked as malicious, but the external command and network behaviors need approval in sensitive environments.
